evidence: Docstring states the conjecture concerns the sum of squares of the first N gaps between consecutive primes, and the statement uses primeGap.
verify_note: decl exists (research open); statement sums primeGap^2 — prime gaps are the subject; QID label matches

evidence: The module header's references link the Wikipedia article on Cramér's conjecture, the context for this Heath-Brown prime-gap-moment conjecture.
verify_note: Cramér's conjecture appears only as a module-header Wikipedia reference; the informal statement is Heath-Brown's gap-moment conjecture — neighboring context, not load-bearing in the statement

evidence: Docstring says Cramér proved this upper bound conditional on the Riemann hypothesis, and the theorem takes RiemannHypothesis as an explicit hypothesis.
verify_note: decl exists (research solved) and takes RiemannHypothesis as an explicit hypothesis; QID matches

evidence: The problem asks about the distribution of normalized gaps (p(n+1) - p n)/log n between consecutive primes, formalized via primeGap.
verify_note: decl exists; statement is about the distribution of primeGap n / log n — prime gaps central; QID matches

evidence: Docstring asks whether the density f c of integers with primeGap n / log n < c exists and is continuous, formalized via HasDensity.
verify_note: decl exists; HasDensity of the gap set is the very question asked; natural density QID matches

evidence: Docstring counts solutions to n = p + 2^k for prime p, so representability by primes is the subject of the problem.
verify_note: decl exists; f counts n = p + 2^k with p prime — primes load-bearing; QID matches

evidence: The statement counts representations n = p + 2^k, i.e. as a prime plus a power of two.
verify_note: decl exists; representation as prime plus a power of two is the statement itself; QID matches

evidence: The problem asks for many consecutive primes below x whose pairwise differences exceed c2, formalized with primeGap.
verify_note: decl exists; statement bounds primeGap over runs of consecutive primes; QID matches

evidence: Docstring: 'Let f: N -> {-1,1} be a multiplicative function' and asks whether its mean value always exists (Wirsing/Halász).
verify_note: decl exists; hypothesis encodes multiplicativity (coprime m n -> f(mn)=f m * f n) — the defining condition of the problem; QID matches

evidence: The problem asks for the maximum size of a B_3 set (all triple sums distinct), the r=3 analogue of a Sidon set; erdosproblems.com tags it 'sidon sets' and cites Green's paper on B_h[g] sets.
verify_note: decl exists; problem is on B_3 sets, the h=3 case of B_h sequences covered by the Sidon-sequence article (Wikipedia has no separate B_h page); genuine mentions

evidence: The theorem states that for every n > 2 there are distinct x < y < z with 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z, which is precisely the Erdős–Straus conjecture (erdosproblems.com/242 comments name it so).
verify_note: decl exists (research open); theorem states 4/n = 1/x+1/y+1/z for all n>2 — this IS the Erdős–Straus conjecture; QID denotes exactly this conjecture

evidence: The statement expresses 4/n as a sum of three unit fractions 1/x + 1/y + 1/z; erdosproblems.com tags the problem 'unit fractions'.
verify_note: decl exists; statement decomposes 4/n into three unit fractions; QID matches

evidence: Schinzel's generalisation asks for a/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z, a decomposition into three unit fractions (Sierpiński 'fractions primaires' reference).
verify_note: decl exists (research open); a/n as a sum of three unit fractions — load-bearing; QID matches

evidence: The conclusion a_n = a_{n-1}^2 - a_{n-1} + 1 is the Sylvester's-sequence recurrence, and the problem's OEIS reference A000058 is Sylvester's sequence.
verify_note: decl exists; conclusion a_n = a_{n-1}^2 - a_{n-1} + 1 is exactly the Sylvester-sequence recurrence (A000058); QID matches

evidence: Docstring: 'Romanoff [Ro34] proved that the answer is yes if C is an integer' — the decl states Romanov's theorem that integers of the form p + C^k have positive lower density for integer C > 1.
verify_note: decl exists (research solved); states positive lower density of {p + C^k} for integer C>1 — precisely Romanov's theorem; QID denotes exactly this theorem

evidence: The open problem asks whether Romanov's positive-density conclusion for p + floor(C^k) extends to all real C > 1.
verify_note: decl exists; the open question is precisely whether Romanov's conclusion extends to real C>1 (the file's own docstring names Romanoff as the integer case) — load-bearing mentions

evidence: The statement asks whether the set {p + floor(C^k)} has positive lower density, formalized via Set.lowerDensity.
